cards.back: Under two minutes? Do it now — tracking costs more than doing.
Sort by urgent×important: do, schedule, delegate, delete.
25 min focus + 5 min break = one pomodoro; short sprints beat procrastination.
Shrink step one until it can't fail ("just open the file").
Give a task a fixed slot, then stop — limits perfectionism and sprawl.
Task switching has a hidden reload cost; one thing at a time is faster.
Do the hardest important task first, before willpower drains.
Work expands to fill the time given; a tighter deadline often still works.
Ten minutes to clear inboxes, check goals, plan the week prevents drift.
Group similar tasks into one block to cut context switching.
Pick 1-3 "most important tasks" each morning; finish those before anything else.
Each night list tomorrow's 6 tasks in priority order; work top-down.
Long, distraction-free focus on one hard task — where the real output happens.
Offload every task to one trusted place so your brain stops re-reminding you.

cards.front: How to use your energy peaks?
cards.back: Schedule hard, creative work when your energy is highest; admin when it dips.
Finishing before starting reduces switching and gets things actually done.
An "if X, then I'll do Y" plan — pre-deciding beats relying on willpower.
Roughly 80% of results come from 20% of efforts — find and protect that 20%.
Real rest (walk, look away) restores focus; scrolling doesn't.
A clear end-of-day routine (review, plan tomorrow) frees your mind to rest.
Routinize small recurring choices (meals, clothes) to save willpower for what matters.
Put your phone out of sight — visible phones cut focus even when silent.

cards.front: Recurring work — how to speed it up?
cards.back: Reuse templates and checklists so you don't re-think solved problems.
